patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking college student/barista. Start with: “I keep getting big bruises and my bleeding won’t stop like it should.” Volunteer: a huge thigh bruise after bumping a table that keeps getting darker/spreading; cuts slow then start bleeding again later; gums bleed when brushing; you’ve always bruised easily since childhood.

If asked about past bleeding: mention prolonged bleeding after a tooth extraction; very heavy, long periods; newborn belly-button bleeding that “wouldn’t stop” (mom’s story); painful swollen lumps after minor bumps lasting weeks; nosebleeds that take a long time.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked about pregnancy or sexual bleeding: early miscarriages; bleeding after sex (you feel embarrassed and fear being judged “dramatic”).

Express misconceptions: you thought it was from a new workout; you read it could be low iron/hormones; you worry about leukemia. Share economic worries only if asked: coverage/insurance fears, assuming EDs always have the right factor, avoiding prophylaxis discussions.

If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear, and become more forthcoming.

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant catridecacog) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO three times daily as needed for mucosal bleeding (max 5 days per episode)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Combined oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30/150 mcg) 1 tablet PO daily
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding (can delay recognition of a coagulation disorder)
Menorrhagia and iron deficiency anemia can be misattributed to gynecologic causes alone
Intermittent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea (increases mucosal bleeding risk and can mimic platelet dysfunction)
Use of combined oral contraceptive (reduces menstrual bleeding but increases baseline thrombotic risk; may bias clinicians away from diagnosing a bleeding disorder)
Recent dental extraction with delayed re-bleeding (can be mistaken for local surgical complication rather than factor XIII deficiency)

required_evidence: History of delayed bleeding or poor wound healing

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact phrase 'congenital factor XIII deficiency' and links it to impaired clot stabilization and bleeding risk.

Considers contraindications/precautions before prescribing (e.g., prior hypersensitivity to FXIII products, thromboembolic history, product-specific issues such as human plasma-derived vs recombinant), and documents rationale if proceeding.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old male warehouse forklift operator with high-school education. You speak vaguely and are somewhat evasive; you minimize problems unless directly asked. Start by saying: “My feet keep tripping me up, and my ankles feel weak.” Volunteer only these upfront: more stumbling on uneven ground/stairs, shoes wearing out on outside edges, toes starting to curl/claw, and that it’s been slowly worsening over years.

If asked specific questions, then admit: hands tire with buttons/jars, nighttime pins-and-needles/burning in feet, thinner calves, can’t run like before, frequent ankle rolls/sprains, worse when tired and you watch your feet, and family members have similar issues; only mention parents are cousins if asked about family history/consanguinity.

Do not volunteer: decreased sensation to cuts/heat, recurrent calluses/pressure spots, balance worse in dark/shower, high-stepping gait, activity avoidance, slow-healing blisters/sores.

Resist discussing intimacy embarrassment, increased nighttime drinking, anxiety/depression unless the clinician is very empathetic and asks gently; then give brief admissions.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s MS, a pinched back nerve, diabetes, or a workout injury. Be anxious about costs and privacy—fear genetic testing/diagnosis will be reported to employer, affect insurance, and that braces/PT won’t be covered. If met with empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the terms "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic" and frames it as a hereditary peripheral neuropathy with typically slow progression.

Elicits and documents key history elements: family history, age of onset, progression tempo, functional limitations (gait, stairs, hand dexterity), falls, pain/paresthesias, and prior exposures/medications that could worsen neuropathy.

Performs/requests a focused neuro-musculoskeletal assessment in narrative: distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss pattern, reflexes, foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes), gait/foot drop, and balance.
States that confirmatory testing is typically via neurology with EMG/NCS and genetic testing; avoids claiming a definitive subtype without appropriate testing.
Communicates that there is no routine curative therapy; emphasizes function-preserving care including physical therapy and orthotics (must include both terms).
Before prescribing any medication, completes allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical) and attempts drug_interaction_check (noncritical); failure of either critical check is a hard fail.

Chooses symptom-directed medications appropriate for neuropathic pain if present and avoids neurotoxic agents when possible; does not prescribe chemotherapy agents (e.g., vincristine) or other clearly inappropriate treatments.
Provides safety counseling relevant to gait instability and sedating medications (fall risk, driving precautions) and offers a follow-up plan with clear return precautions.
Addresses psychosocial aspects: impact on work/school, mobility aids, and offers genetic counseling/family implications discussion in patient-friendly language.
Uses shared decision-making, checks understanding (teach-back), and avoids stigmatizing or deterministic language about disability.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose likely CMT, initiate supportive therapy and symptom control, and refer for confirmation and genetic counseling.
must_collect: gait difficulty/foot drop or frequent tripping
family history of similar symptoms or known neuropathy
foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) or history of ankle sprains
pain severity and characteristics (neuropathic vs musculoskeletal)
comorbidities affecting contraindications (renal disease, severe depression/suicidality for certain agents)
steps: Explain suspected Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as a genetic, inherited peripheral neuropathy with slow progression and variable severity.
Discuss supportive management: physical therapy and orthotics/AFOs; consider OT and fall-prevention strategies.
If neuropathic pain is present, propose a first-line agent (e.g., gabapentin) and perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; perform drug_interaction_check when feasible.
Refer to neurology for EMG/NCS and genetic testing discussion; refer to genetic counseling; refer to orthotics/PM&R for bracing and gait optimization.
Provide follow-up timing and red-flag return precautions (rapid progression, marked asymmetry, bowel/bladder changes, new severe back pain, systemic symptoms).

acceptable_variants.description: Nonpharmacologic-first approach when pain is mild/absent, focusing on PT/orthotics and referral without prescribing analgesics.
steps: Explain CMT as a genetic peripheral neuropathy and set expectations about progression and goals of care.
Initiate physical therapy and orthotics planning; recommend footwear/foot care and fall prevention.
Defer medications due to minimal pain; still perform medication safety review if any new meds are considered.
Refer to neurology and genetic counseling for confirmation and family planning implications.
Arrange follow-up to reassess function, falls, and pain; provide red flags.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative neuropathic pain medication selection (e.g., duloxetine) with appropriate safety screening and counseling.
steps: Explain diagnosis and genetic nature; discuss confirmatory testing pathway.
Recommend physical therapy and orthotics; add OT if hand weakness limits ADLs.

acceptable_variants.description: Broader differential recorded verbally before committing to CMT, while still proceeding with referral and supportive care if CMT is most likely.
steps: Acknowledge differential for chronic distal neuropathy (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, CIDP, toxin/medication-induced neuropathy) and explain why CMT is most consistent given family history/foot deformities/slow progression.
Explain CMT as genetic and outline confirmation with EMG/NCS and genetic testing via neurology.
Start PT and orthotics; counsel on fall prevention and activity pacing.
If prescribing for neuropathic pain, complete allergy_check and contraindication_check first; attempt drug_interaction_check; provide sedation/fall-risk counseling.
Refer to neurology and genetic counseling; follow-up with clear red flags.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of the primary diagnosis so downstream care can proceed.

nl_assertions: States a diagnosis of type 2 diabetes explicitly.
Explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly terms and explicitly mentions HbA1c as the key diagnostic/monitoring metric.
Explains initial treatment approach and explicitly mentions metformin and lifestyle changes (diet, physical activity, weight management) as core components.
Does not prescribe any medication in an L0 diagnosis-only task (no dosing instructions or initiating therapy), but may discuss that metformin is commonly first-line pending clinician confirmation.

Before any prescribing is attempted, performs a contraindication check (critical) and also checks allergies and drug interactions (non-critical) if prescribing is discussed; if unable to verify, clearly defers prescribing and advises verification.

Uses uncertainty appropriately: if criteria for diabetes are not fully established in the presented information, recommends confirming with HbA1c and/or repeat testing per standard practice while still addressing likely diagnosis when supported.
Provides clear follow-up advice (e.g., arrange confirmatory testing, discuss results, diabetes education) without using WRITE tools beyond record_diagnosis.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose type 2 diabetes and communicate diagnosis and initial management concepts without initiating treatment orders.
must_collect: HbA1c value or prior diabetes testing history
steps: Elicit key hyperglycemia symptoms and diabetes risk factors; ask about prior HbA1c/glucose results.
Explain that type 2 diabetes is diagnosed/monitored with HbA1c (and/or glucose testing) and what HbA1c represents.
Discuss first-line management principles: lifestyle changes and that metformin is commonly used as initial medication (without prescribing).

If medication initiation is contemplated, verbally confirm the need for contraindication check first (renal function/advanced CKD, severe illness/hypoxia, alcohol misuse risk, etc.), plus allergy and interaction review; if not verifiable, defer prescribing.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis made with emphasis on confirming criteria and arranging confirmatory testing when HbA1c is not available, while still providing required education about HbA1c and first-line therapy concepts.
steps: Assess symptoms/risk factors and clarify whether any HbA1c or fasting glucose values exist.
Explain type 2 diabetes and explicitly describe HbA1c as a 2–3 month average glucose measure used for diagnosis/monitoring.

Explain treatment overview including lifestyle changes and metformin as typical first-line medication, while stating that starting medication requires contraindication/allergy/interaction checks and lab review (e.g., renal function).
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis plus brief safety-netting about red flags for hyperglycemic crisis and when to seek urgent care, without adding additional WRITE tool usage.
steps: Elicit symptoms and screen for severe hyperglycemia red flags (vomiting, abdominal pain, confusion, dehydration).
Explain type 2 diabetes and HbA1c; provide basic interpretation framing (higher HbA1c = higher average glucose).
Explain treatment overview: lifestyle changes and metformin as common first-line; note prescribing requires contraindication/allergy/interaction checks.
Advise urgent care if severe symptoms suggest hyperglycemic emergency.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 56-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking accounting manager. Start by saying: “I keep getting these sudden, electric-shock pains in my lower jaw on the right side.” Volunteer that the pain comes in brief bursts and can be triggered by touching your face or brushing your teeth.

If asked about pattern/triggers, add: it can happen dozens of times a day on bad days, then ease up; chewing, talking, or a cool breeze can trigger it; between attacks you feel normal or mildly sore; OTC pain meds don’t help; a parent had similar sharp facial pain.

Do not volunteer at first that you’ve been chewing only on the left, avoiding washing that side, eating less, feeling on edge, or sleeping worse—share only if asked about impact, mood, sleep, or daily habits.

If asked specifically about oral hygiene, reluctantly admit you’ve sometimes skipped brushing/flossing because you’re scared and embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you worried it was a bad tooth, ear infection, or sinus issue.

If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it helps and you’re anxious about cost, time off work, and unnecessary tests/surgery.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is accurate identification and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits key trigeminal neuralgia features: recurrent brief (seconds to 2 minutes) unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, severe/electric shock-like quality, triggered by light touch/chewing/talking/brushing teeth, with pain-free intervals.

Agent screens for red flags/secondary causes and atypical features (persistent pain between attacks, sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, age <40, neurologic deficits, hearing/vision changes) and states that these warrant further evaluation (e.g., MRI) rather than assuming classic trigeminal neuralgia.

Agent includes a focused cranial nerve/neurologic assessment plan in reasoning (even if not performed via tools) and differentiates from dental pathology, TMJ disorder, sinusitis, cluster headache, and postherpetic neuralgia based on history.
Agent communicates the diagnosis explicitly using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

If discussing treatment, agent explains first-line therapy is carbamazepine and explicitly mentions "carbamazepine" and "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_treatment), while clarifying that prescribing is not required for this L1 task.

Before any prescribing discussion that implies an imminent prescription, agent performs/requests required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check (all critical, before prescribing).
Agent provides safety-net advice: seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, facial numbness/weakness, severe persistent headache, fever, vision changes, or atypical/worsening pattern.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose classic trigeminal neuralgia based on characteristic paroxysmal unilateral shock-like facial pain with triggers and no neurologic deficits; record diagnosis.
must_collect: attack duration (seconds to 2 minutes) and recurrence
triggers (touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind)
absence of sensory loss or focal neurologic deficits
red flags (bilateral symptoms, persistent pain, numbness, weakness, vision changes)
steps: Take targeted history focusing on trigeminal neuralgia phenotype and triggers.
Assess for atypical features/red flags suggesting secondary trigeminal neuralgia or alternative diagnosis; state need for further evaluation if present.
Explain diagnosis to patient using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" and brief rationale.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent diagnoses trigeminal neuralgia and additionally recommends (without ordering via tools) MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve when atypical features are present or in younger patients, while still recording the diagnosis.
steps: Elicit classic TN symptom pattern and screen for red flags/secondary causes.
Explain trigeminal neuralgia diagnosis and discuss that imaging (MRI) may be needed if atypical features/young age/neurologic findings.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent explains first-line treatment conceptually (carbamazepine) but does not prescribe (consistent with L1), and emphasizes required safety checks would be done before prescribing.
steps: Elicit classic TN features and exclude red flags.
Explain trigeminal neuralgia diagnosis.
Explain treatment options, explicitly mentioning carbamazepine for trigeminal neuralgia and noting allergy/contraindication/interaction checks are required before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) as the working diagnosis, enabling appropriate escalation and management.

nl_assertions: States a working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) and frames it as a severe acute diffuse lung injury causing low oxygen.
Explains diagnosis to the patient using the terms "acute interstitial pneumonia" and "low oxygen" (or equivalent hypoxemia language).
Explains treatment using the terms "acute interstitial pneumonia" and "steroids" and clarifies that treatment is typically supportive and often requires hospital/ICU-level care.
Assesses severity and flags red-flag features requiring urgent escalation (e.g., severe dyspnea, resting hypoxemia, cyanosis, altered mental status, hemodynamic instability).

Elicits key history to distinguish AIP from mimics: infectious symptoms/exposures, aspiration risk, inhalational/toxin exposures, medication/drug exposures, autoimmune symptoms, recent travel/sick contacts.

Includes an appropriate differential for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (e.g., ARDS due to infection, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, pulmonary embolism, acute eosinophilic pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, organizing pneumonia).
Avoids unsafe reassurance; communicates uncertainty appropriately and emphasizes need for prompt evaluation and monitoring.

If discussing any medication plan (even hypothetically), performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check are treated as critical; drug interaction check is performed or acknowledged as needed.

Does not prescribe antibiotics as definitive therapy for AIP without rationale; if infection cannot be excluded, frames empiric antibiotics as coverage while evaluating, not as the primary diagnosis-specific treatment.
Provides clear follow-up/return precautions focused on worsening breathing, increasing oxygen needs, chest pain, syncope, confusion, or inability to speak in full sentences.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize AIP as the likely diagnosis in a patient with acute progressive hypoxemic respiratory failure and diffuse interstitial/alveolar involvement, explain it clearly, and document the diagnosis.
must_collect: low oxygen (hypoxemia) symptoms or measured low SpO2
absence of known chronic interstitial lung disease history
VTE risk factors/pleuritic chest pain to consider PE
steps: Assess clinical severity and immediate risk (work of breathing, mental status, ability to speak, SpO2 if available) and recommend urgent ED/hospital evaluation if hypoxemic or in distress.
Elicit focused history for AIP and key alternative causes (infection, cardiogenic edema, PE, eosinophilic pneumonia, DAH, organizing pneumonia, drug/toxin exposure).
Explain to the patient that acute interstitial pneumonia is an acute, severe inflammation/injury of the lung tissue that can cause low oxygen and may progress quickly.

Discuss typical management at a high level (supportive oxygen/ventilation; steroids are often used though evidence is limited; treat triggers if identified) without issuing a prescription in this L1 task.
Record the working diagnosis as acute interstitial pneumonia and document key supporting features and leading differentials.

acceptable_variants.description: Documents AIP as probable while explicitly noting ARDS due to infection as a close alternative and emphasizing need to exclude infection and cardiac causes urgently.
steps: Identify acute hypoxemic respiratory failure pattern and state AIP vs ARDS/infection as leading considerations.
Explain AIP and low oxygen in plain language and communicate uncertainty/need for urgent evaluation.
List differentials (pneumonia/viral ARDS, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, PE, eosinophilic pneumonia, DAH) and what would help distinguish them.
Record diagnosis as probable AIP (or AIP/ARDS spectrum) with differentials documented.

acceptable_variants.description: If the agent begins to outline a steroid plan, it still remains acceptable provided it performs allergy and contraindication checks before any prescribing and frames steroids as specialist-directed/hospital-based therapy.
steps: Explain AIP and low oxygen; recommend ED/hospital evaluation.
Discuss that steroids are commonly used for acute interstitial pneumonia in hospital settings; emphasize risks and need for monitoring.
Before any prescription attempt: perform allergy check and contraindication check (and drug interaction check if feasible) and document the results in narrative.
Record diagnosis as AIP and advise urgent escalation.
